MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Mar-18-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Mirna J. Scheffy of HAWKINS PARNELL & YOUNG (415-766-3225) appearing through CourtCall for defendant Brystol-Myers Squibb Company.
Albert Oganesyan of SIMON GREENSTONE PANATIER BARTLETT, P.C. (562-590-3400), appearing through CourtCall for plaintiff Dalal Metwally.
Lisa M. Barley and Mary Rahmes of SIMON GREENSTONE PANATIER BARTLETT, P.C. (562-590-3400), appearing through CourtCall for plaintiff Dalal Metwally.

Matter heard simultaneously with Defendant Revlon Consumer Products Corporation's Motion for Summary Judgment OR, ALTERNATIVELY, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

RULING:
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Brystol-Myers Squibb Company's Motion for Summary Judgment is denied. Defendant BMS has failed to sustain its initial burden of production pursuant to Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 854-855. Defendant failed to produce evidence showing that "plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence" to establish that Defendant's products exposed Plaintiff to asbestos. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co., supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 854.) Plaintiffs' Responses to Defendant's Requests for Production, Set One, specify the evidence that would be used in support of Plaintiff's allegations. (Scheffy Decl., Ex. G, Response to Request for Production No. 8.) Defendant relies on Plaintiff's deposition testimony that she does not know whether talc was used in the powders or source of the talc used. It cannot be inferred from Plaintiff's own ignorance that Plaintiff will be unable to produce other evidence or testimony proving asbestos-content. (See Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.) Additionally, Defendant's argument that Plaintiff's document production and witness identification is "factually devoid" fails. Defendant has failed to discuss any documents referenced by Plaintiff in detail. (See Scheffy Decl., Ex. G, Response to Request for Production No. 8.) Ultimately, Defendant's argument fails to explain how Plaintiff's discovery responses support the contention that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, evidence proving the element of causation. (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 103, citing Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield, supra, 25 Cal.4th 826, Saelzler v. Advanced Group 400 (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763 [""[A] defendant cannot simply 'argue' that a plaintiff lacks sufficient evidence to establish causation; the defendant must make an affirmative 'showing' that the plaintiff cannot do so"].)
Finally, Defendant BMS argument that Plaintiff cannot succeed on a punitive damage claim fails to explain how Plaintiff's discovery responses support the contention that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, evidence proving the element of causation. (Scheffy Decl., Ex. D, Response to Interrogatory No. 1; and Ex. G, Response to Request for Production No. 8 ) (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 103, citing Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield, supra, 25 Cal.4th 826, Saelzler v. Advanced Group 400 (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763 [""[A] defendant cannot simply 'argue' that a plaintiff lacks sufficient evidence to establish causation; the defendant must make an affirmative 'showing' that the plaintiff cannot do so"].) Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Reporter: Deborah Morin, CSR #11558, dmorincsr@gmail.com (Tel: 626-221-6397). (503/CML)

Defendant Revlon has failed to sustain the initial burden of production pursuant to Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 854-855. (Defendant's Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Nos. 10-12.) Defendant has failed to produce evidence proving that "plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence" to establish that Defendant's products exposed Plaintiff to asbestos. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co., supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 854.) Specifically, Plaintiff's Responses to Defendant's Requests for Production, Set One, specify the evidence that would be used in support of Plaintiff's allegations. (See Scheffy Decl., Ex. G, Response to Request for Production No. 9.) Further, while Defendant relies on Plaintiff's deposition testimony wherein, she states that she does not know whether talc was used in the powders or the source of the talc used, it cannot be inferred from Plaintiff's own ignorance that Plaintiff will be unable to produce other evidence or testimony proving asbestos-content. (See Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.)
Defendant's argument that Plaintiff's document production and witness identification if "factually devoid" fails. Defendant fails to discuss any documents or witnesses referenced by Plaintiff in detail. (Scheffy Decl., Ex. G, Response to Request for Production No. 9.) The documents do not support Defendant's contention that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, evidence proving the element of causation. (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 103, citing Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield, supra, 25 Cal.4th 826, Saelzler v. Advanced Group 400 (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763 [""[A] defendant cannot simply 'argue' that a plaintiff lacks sufficient evidence to establish causation; the defendant must make an affirmative 'showing' that the plaintiff cannot do so"].)
Finally, Defendant Revlon fails to sustain its initial burden of production on the punitive damages claim. Defendant Revlon has not demonstrated that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, evidence proving the element of causation. (Scheffy Decl., Ex. D, Response to Interrogatory No. 1; and Ex. G, Response to Request for Production No. 9) (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 103, citing Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield, supra, 25 Cal.4th 826, Saelzler v. RULING:
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendants Puig North America, Inc. and Antonio Puig S.A.'s Motion to Quash Service of Summons or Stay or Dismiss is off calendar for failure to lodge courtesy copies with the Court pursuant to Local Rule 2.7B. Defendants Puig North America, Inc. and Antonio Puig S.A.'s Motion is also untimely per Code of Civil Procedure section 1005, subdivision (b). On November 18, 2020, the Clerk's Office rejected the moving papers for failure to include a handwritten signature on the Proof of Service as required by Local Rule 2.11M(2). On November 25, 2020, Defendants Puig North America, Inc. and Antonio Puig S.A. re-filed the moving papers, but did not re-notice the Motion. Therefore, the Motion was filed with only thirteen court days' notice. Prevailing party is to submit a proposed order in compliance with C.R.C. 3.1312. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction; Declaration Of Elizabeth N. Branham
Off Calendar Oct-22-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT AVON PRODUCTS, INC.'s Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction

Appearances:
Mary T. Rahmes of SIMON GREENSTONE PANATIER, (562-590-3400), appearing for Plaintiff Dalal Metwally.
Bill D. Fountain of HAWKINS PARNELL THACKSTON & YOUNG (415-766-3202) appearing for defendant Avon Products, Inc.
Trina M. Clayton of LEWIS BRISBOIS (415-362-2580), for defendant Christian Dior.

Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted:

Defendant Avon Products, Inc.'s Motion to Quash for Lack of Personal Jurisdiction is DENIED. Plaintiff has demonstrated (1) that Defendant has purposefully established contacts with California, and (2) that her claims arise out of or are related to any forum-directed activities. (Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. v. Superior Court of California, San Francisco Cty. (2017) 137 S. Ct. 1773, 1785.) Plaintiff's evidence shows that Avon owned and operated Avon Products distribution facilities in Monrovia and Pasadena between 1980 to 2013 for which it paid property taxes and insurance, Avon had Sales Centers and Training Centers throughout the state between 1980 and 2002, and Avon had three California First Interstate Bank accounts and one Citibank Delaware bank account for expenses at the Pasadena Branch including payroll, with balances totaling over $24 million between September 1986 and March 1987. (see, e.g., Plaintiff's Ex. Q [Deposition of Defendant Avon Products Inc.'s PMK, Karen Abravanel at 54-28; 80-81, 170; 174-176; 213; 224; Ex. R].) Defendant has not shown that the exercise of jurisdiction would be unreasonable.

Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: S. Le. Reporter: December Moore, CSR #8718, dmoore@bayareatrialreporters.com (415-328-6644). (503/CML)
